stem: To test \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]n^{2} + [[b]]n + [[c]]}{[[d]]n^{3} + [[e]]n} \) by limit comparison against the harmonic series \( b_n = \dfrac{1}{n} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{a_n}{b_n} \).
solution: Multiplying out, \( \dfrac{a_n}{b_n} = \dfrac{n([[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n+[[c]])}{[[d]]n^{3}+[[e]]n} = \dfrac{[[a]]n^{3}+[[b]]n^{2}+[[c]]n}{[[d]]n^{3}+[[e]]n} \); both numerator and denominator have degree \( 3 \), so the limit is the ratio of leading coefficients \( L = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \). Since \( 0<L<\infty \), the limit comparison test says \( \sum a_n \) behaves like \( \sum b_n \) (both diverge, since \( \sum 1/n \) diverges).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This assumes the terms vanish in the ratio, but the degrees match so the limit is a nonzero constant.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: To test \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{[[a]]n^{3} + [[b]]n}} \) by limit comparison against \( b_n = \dfrac{1}{n^{3/2}} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{a_n}{b_n} \).
solution: \( \dfrac{a_n}{b_n} = \dfrac{n^{3/2}}{\sqrt{[[a]]n^{3}+[[b]]n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{n^{3}}{[[a]]n^{3}+[[b]]n}} \to \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{[[a]]}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{[[a]]}} = [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \). Since \( 0<L<\infty \), \( \sum a_n \) behaves like \( \sum 1/n^{3/2} \), which converges (a convergent p-series with \( p=3/2 \)).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the reciprocal, using \( \sqrt{a} \) instead of \( 1/\sqrt{a} \).
This forgets the square root, using \( 1/a \) instead of \( 1/\sqrt{a} \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign; both \( a_n \) and \( b_n \) are eventually positive, so \( L \) must be positive.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This uses \( a-1 \) under the square root instead of \( a \).
